data.title: Given The Nvidia Tax, Broadcom And Marvell Offer A Powerful 1-2 AI Punch
data.description:
A couple of months back, I reviewed Nvidia and came away with a hold rating despite being impressed on various accounts. The challenge, in my opinion, is that Nvidia’s (NASDAQ:NVDA) net income margins are so high and its stranglehold on AI compute is so strong that it will almost inevitably create pushback from AI developers, like Google and OpenAI. We’re seeing this play out with various efforts to launch competing chips, including in-house chips for Google and the like. For now, Nvidia is likely to maintain its moat, but the competition is making headway.

Ultimately, the "Nvidia tax" will continue to fuel efforts to diversify AI chips, which in turn should provide Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) and Marvell (NASDAQ:MRVL) with strong, sustained tailwinds. If nothing else, more chip options will keep Nvidia honest and will likely trim net income margins over time, although ultimately, I do believe the chip industry will diversify and Broadcom and Marvell should be among the biggest winners.

There is a lot to like with both Broadcom and Marvell and I could justify investing in both. That said, there are a few serious concerns with Broadcom's valuation and fundamentals. With Marvell, I see no major hangups. Also, Broadcom is a far larger and more diversified company, making it less of a pure play and likely weakening the impact of the Nvidia tax. Marvell is more of an AI chip and data center infrastructure pure play and I reckon it has higher upside, but also a greater risk profile and lower floor. Given the speed and potential for the Artificial Intelligence industry, I’m comfortable taking on higher risks in exchange for higher upside. As a smaller company, if Marvell can tap into the opportunities provided by the Nvidia tax, it could move the needle substantially.

Overall, I believe Marvell offers the better investment opportunity. I don't think Broadcom is a bad investment, per se, and I can see why investors may want to invest in both. Doing so would offer an opportunity to hedge against risks. These companies clash in certain battlegrounds, such as designing custom chips. If one does begin to pull ahead at the expense of the other, share price gains for the leading company could offset share price declines for the laggard. Still, in terms of potential performance and fundamentals, Marvell takes the lead in my book.

The New Tea Party? Dumping the Nvidia Tax

It’s pretty much impossible to not be impressed by Nvidia’s fundamentals. Nvidia’s net income margin currently tops 50%, while its gross profit margin comes in at 70%. Since the gross profit margin doesn’t include marketing costs and the like, the relatively narrow spread between the net income margin and gross income margin suggests that Nvidia doesn’t have to put as many resources into non-COGS expenses as is typical. The ongoing chip shortages mean that Nvidia can’t produce enough to fully meet demand. Going back to economics 101, when demand outstrips supply, prices tend to go up.

Most sources point to Nvidia controlling 90% or more of the AI training market. One source claims that Nvidia’s AI training market share has declined from 72% in 2023 to 58% in 2025, with AMD controlling 22% and custom-chips (e.g. TPUs) accounting for 18% (2025). Market share data can be murky in many industries, and companies working on AI in particular often remain intentionally vague. I’d be careful about assigning too much credence to any specific numbers, but no matter what way you cut it, Nvidia is the dominant AI chip provider.

Right now, one of the best Nvidia challengers is Alphabet, which uses AI-specific Tensor Processing Units (TPUs) both for training and inference. Alphabet believes that its TPUs could take 10% of Nvidia’s sales. Reportedly, OpenAI at one point threatened to switch to Alphabet TPUs, forcing Nvidia to cut prices by 30%. (There are reports of this, but I wouldn’t treat it as confirmed.) Alphabet’s chips were designed in partnership with Broadcom. UBS reports that Alphabet TPUs will likely account for $30 billion in revenue for Broadcom this year. Further, shipments of TPUs will grow from 3.7 million this year to 5 million in 2027. As of 2025, however, only about 31% of Broadcom's revenues come from AI. Thus, even as AI revenues grow, the overall impact on Broadcom will be weakened to some extent. Broadcom derives over 40% of its revenues from software, including cybersecurity and automation software.

Broadcom's partnership with Alphabet is the most attractive card in its hand. However, it's been rumored in the past that Alphabet would switch to Marvell as its primary supplier and partner. This has yet to come to pass, but I suspect that Alphabet used the threat as leverage (similar to how OpenAI used Alphabet as leverage against Nvidia). Alphabet has brought on a new partner in MediaTek to handle some aspects of the TPU. This move was made to diversify Alphabet's supply chain and lower costs. I wouldn't be surprised either if Marvell does eventually get looped in. Thus, while the partnership with Alphabet is enticing, it also leaves me wary.

Marvell, meanwhile, is more heavily concentrated in AI and data centers, accounting for roughly three-quarters of the company's revenues. As such, the massive AI CapEx boom and efforts to reduce the Nvidia tax could dramatically move the needle. Marvell has partnered with Amazon to help design and produce its custom Inferentia and Trainium chips. With Amazon looking to rapidly expand its AI footprint with $200 billion in CapEx spending this year, Marvell could be in line for generous paydays. Amazon is mostly using these chips for internal applications as far as I can tell. Anthropic does use Amazon’s chips, but they also use Alphabet TPUs and Nvidia chips.

As with Alphabet, Amazon is also looking to diversify its supply chain and bring in more partners. This should lower costs and provide leverage for Amazon. Rumors circulated in December 2025 that Marvell was losing Amazon's Trainium 3 and 4 designs. It was also claimed that Microsoft was moving to Broadcom. Microsoft is Marvell's second biggest customer, and it may also be looking to diversify its supply chain, but that doesn't mean Marvell will lose all of its business. Marvell's CEO refuted both accounts, and analysts at JP Morgan have echoed the claims. That said, AIchip Technologies is being brought in to handle at least some of the work with Amazon's AI chips.

Custom chips, including Alphabet's and Amazon’s chips, tend to be more energy efficient because they are designed specifically for AI. Nvidia’s AI chips evolved from more general GPUs popular in gaming and the like. Energy costs could influence the equation and encourage custom-chip adoption. While I am focusing on chips in this article, both Broadcom and Marvell provide other products and services. Marvell is a leading Digital Signal Processor provider, for example, enabling rapid data transfer within AI centers.

Broadcom and Marvell Fundamentals and Valuation Considerations

As far as fundamentals are concerned, I believe both Broadcom and Marvell are generally pretty attractive, but MRVL comes away with some edges in key areas. I’ll also compare both stocks to Nvidia. Seeking Alpha’s quant tools give both companies F grades when it comes to valuation and I can see why the data points that way. However, we live in an era of high valuations. That said, Broadcom’s ~72 PE GAAP TTM ratio, shown above, is especially high. Marvell looks like a downright bargain compared to both Nvidia and Broadcom. Both Broadcom and Nvidia have had far higher PE ratios before, as illustrated below.

Another area of concern valuation wise is the Price/Sales ratio. Broadcom and Nvidia both have PS ratios of about 25, which is exceptionally high. Marvell is far more reasonable at 9, which is a touch high but pretty normal for a tech company. Broadcom would be an easy buy for me with a PS ratio of around 10 and a PE ratio of around 30. Unfortunately, however, that's not the case in the real world, and while I believe Broadcom has upside, it's not as extensive as Marvell's.

All three companies are enjoying strong revenue growth, but Marvell is growing much more quickly. Broadcom has the lowest growth, under 30%. Granted, Broadcom is a much larger company than Marvell. The lower baseline with Marvell can distort growth in some ways, and it’s often harder for larger companies to secure rapid growth in terms of percentages. Nvidia is an exception to this, of course, but no one should be surprised by that.

Nvidia also enjoys the highest net income margin. Indeed, its 53% net income margin is extremely high for a company of such size. Marvell and Broadcom aren’t slouches, and they’re highly profitable even by the high standards of tech stocks, but they lag far behind Nvidia. Yet that’s also why I am wary of the Nvidia tax and why I believe Marvell and Broadcom will enjoy tailwinds. Especially with Broadcom and given Alphabet's moves to bring in MediaTek, I would recommend keeping a close eye on profitability. Broadcom may need to shrink its margins with Alphabet's business in order to remain in its good graces. Right now, the high profit margins should be priced into Broadcom's share prices, so if margins do shrink, it could have an outsized impact.

Broadcom does have a bit more debt than I wanted to see, especially compared to the other two. The debt doesn’t strike me as a serious risk, but it needs to be taken into account with the valuation. All in all, these three companies strike me as healthy as far as the balance sheet goes.

The quant tools give all three companies a hold rating, with valuation getting poor marks across the board. My fellow Seeking Alpha analysts are more optimistic with buy ratings across the board. While these stocks aren’t cheap and the valuations are high, so long as the AI race continues, I believe Broadcom and Marvell will rise. Still, given Marvell's more favorable valuation metrics and its position as an AI pure play, if I had to pick one stock or the other, I'd go with MRVL. I’m a bit wary of Nvidia owing to the Nvidia tax and last rated it as a hold a few months ago.

Why I Could Be Wrong

The strongest part of Nvidia’s moat, in my opinion, isn’t actually its hardware but instead its CUDA software. From what I understand, this software is especially crucial in the training phase and is the most widely used platform. Switching to a new platform would be immensely difficult and would consume a lot of resources. Many are concerned that the AI race is evolving into a bubble. If this turns out to be true, Marvell and Broadcom would likely rank among the hardest hit. Broadcom is more diversified in some ways, and so that might mitigate some of the damage. However, Broadcom is still largely focused on AI data centers and would be hit hard.

Both Marvell and Broadcom are dependent on a small number of customers. As a result, said customers have a lot of leverage, and clearly, some are looking to rein in costs. If either company loses a major contract, it could tank their share prices. Right now, the AI race is so white hot and resources across the industry are stretched so thin that I believe this risk is muted. However, margins may come under pressure.

Further, I’d be careful about writing China off. As of now, it’s believed that China is well behind the United States in terms of chip design and the like. However, China does have a lot of resources, and when the Chinese government prioritizes something, they often find success. While I doubt China will catch up any time soon, I wouldn’t be shocked if they mount a stronger-than-expected challenge.

Key Takeaway: MRVL Takes The Edge

So long as the AI race continues at the current pace, I believe Broadcom and Marvell will tick upward. Still, Broadcom's PE and PS ratios are substantially higher than I'd want to see, and I believe its margins will come under pressure more quickly owing to Alphabet bringing MediaTek on board. The AI race is drawing in so much cash that I could justify buying Broadcom stocks, but overall, I believe Marvell has the edge. As an AI pure play, I also expect the AI tailwinds to have a larger impact on Marvell.

As for Nvidia, I suspect custom chips will nibble away at its market share going forward. If the CUDA moat is ever breached, Nvidia could come under immense pressure (that's a big if, however).

data.title: Too Many Worries Exist To Get On Board With TFI International
data.description:
Right now, the logistics industry is facing challenges. The COVID-19 pandemic created a boon for the space. This was because of a flurry of buying activity caused by worries over supply chain constraints. Significant investments were made in the trucking sector. And now that we have reached a more normalized state following the end of the pandemic, there remains an oversupply of capacity and, interestingly enough, a shortage of truck drivers has now formed. Add on top of this the reality of tariffs and a slowdown in the US economy, and sprinkle on top new fears about policy changes that could further restrict the space, and investors are wise to be cautious right now.

One prospect in this space that investors should be aware of is TFI International (TFII). Even though shares of the company are up so far this year, they are still down 7.1% over the last 12 months. The latest hit to the company came on February 12 when shares closed down 8.1%. This development was largely associated with worries over AI and the disruption that could occur because of it in the transportation sector. While this is not something that investors should ignore completely, I would say that larger fundamental issues need to be focused on instead. And when you look at the data, you see a business that is priced at decent multiples, but not at ones that are low enough to justify a bullish outlook at this time.

My caution regarding the company is informed not only by the historical trajectory that we have seen recently. It's also based on concerns about what the future holds. Right now, analysts seem pessimistic about the business. Management is expected to announce financial results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year on February 17th. And as we approach that date, analysts believe that revenue and earnings will fall year over year. The drop in earnings could be quite sizable. But that makes sense for an industry going through the types of challenges that the trucking space is, especially considering how low margin and asset intensive this industry happens to be.

Taking TFI International for a drive

The management team at TFI International describes the company as a major player in the North American transportation and logistics space. Although it is a Canadian company, most of its revenue comes from the US. In fact, for the first nine months of the 2025 fiscal year, 69.6% of overall revenue came from the US, with the remaining 30.4% attributable to Canada. This is a diverse operator in a couple of different respects. For starters, as the image above illustrates, it touches on several industries. The greatest exposure that it has is to retail, followed by manufactured goods. But other major industries include building materials, the automotive space, metals and mining, food and beverage, and more. The company caters to these markets through three distinct operating segments, each of which deserves some attention.

The first of these is its Less-Than-Truckload segment. This is, to me, one of the most interesting aspects of the transportation and logistics industry today. Instead of providing full load services for customers, it essentially engages in the pickup, consolidation, transportation, and delivery of smaller loads. Conceptually, this market is more attractive if your goal is to focus on margins. And this is because, as opposed to essentially buying access to a truck, you are instead buying access to the network that makes this kind of shipping possible at scale. Network effects almost always generate more in the way of profit margin, especially when customers are smaller and have less negotiating power.

Then we have the Truckload segment. This part of the company focuses on providing full loads that are carried from the customer to the destination designated for the load in question. And finally, we have the Logistics segment. This is an asset light suite of services that includes, amongst other things, brokerage, freight forwarding, and transportation management solutions. The company also puts under this segment its small package parcel delivery services.

As I mentioned at the start of this article, financial performance for the business has been a bit rough recently. And that is because of challenging market conditions. In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years. Even though revenue ultimately expanded from 2023 to 2024, net profits have been declining consistently. Back during the pandemic and shortly after, logistics companies had tremendous negotiating power. But because of over investment in capacity and a return to more normal levels of buying activity, that pricing power evaporated. Now it is true that cash flows for the company have actually increased. But even that is nuanced. For instance, EBITDA for the business was lower in 2024 than it was in 2022.

Instead of dwelling too much on those changes, I think our efforts would be better focused on more recent developments. In the chart above, you can see results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time a year earlier. Revenue of $5.97 billion fell short of the $6.32 billion the company reported a year earlier. Even if we strip out fuel surcharges, we would get a decline from $5.48 billion to $5.23 billion. With that drop in sales came a decline in net income from $334.4 million to $238.9 million. On an adjusted basis, we would get a drop from $383.5 million to $275.4 million. Operating cash flow for the company contracted from $800.3 million to $695.6 million. Stripping out changes and working capital, we get a drop from $807.6 million to $609.1 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business declined from $1.01 billion to $890.9 million.

This drop in revenue for the company came about because of weakness involving two of the three operating segments of the business. In the image above, you can see precisely what I mean. Revenue before fuel surcharges for the Less-Than-Truckload segment fell from $2.35 billion to $2.07 billion. There are multiple drivers behind this. However, a lot of the pain came from the company’s U.S. LTL business, with revenue plunging 11.5% from $1.46 billion to $1.29 billion. Weak demand, driven by the impact of high interest rates, inflationary pressures, and consumer confidence levels that are near all time lows, can be blamed for this. According to management, shipments ultimately dropped 9.9% while revenue per shipment, excluding fuel costs, dropped 1.8% from $338.84 to $332.58. Its Canadian LTL business actually saw a 1.9% increase in revenue per shipment. But overall shipments there dropped 9.7% year over year.

The management team at the company acknowledged that, even heading into the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, the environment was slow, with volume coming in weak. Talking about the freight recession that we still find ourselves in, they said that after three years of pain, the company expects that investments in the industrial sector, the prospect of consumers getting tax refunds, and other contributors, could make 2026 better than what 2025 ultimately ended up being.

The other weak spot for the company ended up being its Logistics this segment, with revenue contracting sharply from $1.31 billion to $1.15 billion. This contraction is also a reflection of the overall weakness that the transportation and logistics space is dealing with. Management said that the pain was especially severe in the US, while Canada held up better. It appears as though the government shutdown did not help here. After all, a lot of the revenue that the company generates actually comes from the US Department of Defense. So while management assured investors that the long term outlook would still be positive here, short term, the business dealt with pain.

The only strength for the company came from its Truckload segment. Revenue actually jumped from $1.86 billion to $2.06 billion. This is fantastic and surprising to see. According to the data provided, the company benefited from a surge in its specialized truckload business, with revenue skyrocketing 11.7% from $1.40 billion to $1.56 billion. Actual brokerage revenue moved up 27.3%. According to the company, it benefited from a 13.8% rise in the number of trucks, which helped to push overall activity higher. Revenue per truck, excluding fuel, did fall slightly. But that decline was only 0.9% year over year. As great as the overall move higher is, it's important to note that the rise was not because of a resurgence in demand for the industry. In fact, the increase in revenue was driven by a $312.6 million impact from business acquisitions. Actual organic revenue declined $111.7 million because of lower pricing and a decline in volumes caused by broader market conditions.

The initial fear that swept the transportation and logistics sector the other day centered largely around AI developments. One source indicated that new AI tools risk slashing major freight inefficiencies that could ultimately lead to the lower demand for the services that they provide. At face value, this looks suspicious. After all, improvements from an operational efficiency standpoint it should lead to higher profits and cash flows. But when you have an industry that generates revenue from keeping costly assets engaged, and when those improvements on the efficiency side of things are expected to occur rapidly and could reduce the demand for those assets, you risk an overabundance of supply.

The AI development in question involved a company by the name of Algorhythm Holdings. The tool in question is called SemiCab, and it essentially is a platform that allows operators to scale freight volumes by as much as 400% without increasing headcount. It supposedly reduced empty freight miles by more than 70% across active customer networks. According to the business, trucks are driving empty nearly one out of every three miles, resulting in over $1 trillion in freight spending that is wasteful each year.

Naturally, this does not bode well for the trucking and logistics industry on its own. It definitely creates additional risk for market participants. But this is not the only problem that the company or industry is facing. According to ACT Research, which came out with their own trucking industry forecast for 2026 last month, the trucking sector does seem to be transitioning into an early stage rebalancing act. But it's still not at the point of recovery. The good news is that capacity contraction is accelerating because of weak profitability. But they still expect 2026 to be more of a transition year because of tariffs and broader economic malaise. Other parties are a bit more optimistic. For instance, in early January, C.H. Robinson (CHRW) decided to increase its spot forecast for the trucking sector for 2026. They expect it to now grow by 8% instead of 6%. Having said that, they said that while capacity in the industry has declined, it still is above historical norms. Add on top of this other issues, like imports coming from Asia that are down materially year over year, and it's clear that the space is fragile at best. I personally have been calling for a recession since August of last year. I think that is becoming increasingly likely as well. And if I am correct, that will only exacerbate problems.

We also have the political side of things. Even ignoring the prospect of trade war escalations that could work against the company, you also have the US government taking a hostile stance against workers in the space. Recently, Sean Duffy, the U.S. Transportation Secretary said that the agency is prohibiting ‘unqualified foreign drivers’ from obtaining licenses to drive commercial trucks and buses. This could involve consular screening that would normally only apply to non-US citizens. Already, there exists a shortage of truck drivers of between 60,000 and 80,000 in the US alone. And right now, about one in every six truck drivers are foreign born. This change in policy risks increasing that shortage. And it is something that investors, regardless of their opinions on immigration or the current administration in the US, should watch carefully.

If we use management’s estimate for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year when it comes to adjusted earnings per share, we would get, at the midpoint, adjusted net profits for 2025 in its entirety of $345.3 million. That would be down from the $489.5 million that the company generated a year earlier. And if we project out results based on what we have seen when it comes to the other profitability metrics, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of $919.9 million and EBITDA of $1.17 billion.

Using these estimates, as well as the results of the company achieved for 2024, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced. Although the price to earnings multiple was high, the other trading multiples are actually relatively attractive. Of course, other players in the space trade at or around these levels as well. In that same chart, you can see how the company compares to five other similar enterprises. It's definitely not the most expensive of the group. But it's not the cheapest either.

One thing that investors should be diligent about would be financial performance for the final quarter of 2025 and management’s expectations for 2026. Hopefully, we will get more insight soon. In fact, we are all but guaranteed to. This is because, on February 18th, the company is going to be announcing financial results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. In the table below, you can see that they expect adjusted earnings per share to come in in between $0.80 and $0.90, which compares unfavorably to the $1.19 per share reported a year earlier. That table also shows that analysts expect revenue, earnings per share, and adjusted earnings per share, to all contract. If this comes to fruition, we would likely see cash flows drop also.

Takeaway

Conceptually, I believe that companies like TFI International are appealing. They sit at the core of the American economy. In such a globalized world, you would expect a space like this to do well. And at times throughout history, it has. But right now, there exists a lot of uncertainty. This uncertainty involves not only continued weakness from the economy, but also when it comes to politics and technology. If shares of TFI International were cheaper than they currently are, especially in relation to other similar firms, I would be more optimistic. But for now, the most generous rating that I can give it is a ‘hold’.

data.title: Vale: The Rally Has Outpaced Iron Ore Fundamentals (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In my previous work, I defended Vale's (VALE) bullish thesis based on three key pillars: (1) an iron ore producer improving cost predictability (C1/t) as a structural margin anchor; (2) a favorable short-term iron ore backdrop supported by seasonality and trade détente; and (3) declining Brumadinho/Samarco cash commitments unlocking a stronger FCF and dividend profile.

After reaching the lowest levels at the beginning of last year since the height of COVID-19 in 2020, the performance since then has been a strong rally, with my bullish thesis largely played out with operational execution strengthening and the stock re-rating meaningfully.

As Vale reported 4Q25 numbers on February 12, reiterating another quarter of strong execution, cost predictability in line with consensus, and solid free cash flows, I believe that after the strong rally—much less correlated with recent iron ore movements and more driven by macro flows into Brazil following the sell-off in U.S. equities—Vale's thesis now shifts from execution to cycle, as medium-term iron ore supply dynamics now appear increasingly relevant for assessing incremental upside.

Q4 Was Operationally Strong, But Cleaner Than It Looks

Vale's recent 4Q25 was decent—perhaps even better than it seemed in the preview of operating results released at the end of January—but the market was already anticipating the print.

In this case, the last print brought Vale reporting Proforma EBITDA of $4.8 billion, (+9.9% QoQ; 17.4% YoY), and with VBM (Vale Base Metals) being the highlight: (1) Copper with record production (108Kt); (2) Copper EBITDA at US$1.1 billion; (3) Nickel surprising in operational efficiency (Voisey's Bay and Long Harbor); and (4) Gold helping via price (+20% QoQ).

In addition, Vale reported C1/t at US$21.3/t, which was basically in line with its 2025 guidance, and free cash flows came in at a robust $1.7 billion—flat vs. last year. For 2025, Vale ended with $5.6 billion in FCF, up 3% vs. last year.

On the downside, 4Q25 included a net loss of $3.8 billion. But this was attributable to accounting factors such as a $3.5 billion impairment of nickel and a $2.8 billion tax write-off, having no impact on cash.

In short, I would say that Vale is in one of its best moments in many years, but this does not change the structural dependence on iron ore, which still represents ~86% of its EBITDA generation.

The Real Issue Is 2026–2027: Iron Ore Supply Is Accelerating

There are two major problems that I see for Vale's thesis at the moment, and neither of them has to do with what was posted in 4Q25 or in 2025.

The first is that Vale historically tends to be a forward-looking iron ore cycle thesis. When we look at the chart below showing the movements of spot iron ore, Vale's price, and its correlation, we can see that in recent months, iron ore has been practically flat (+0.43%) and VALE has jumped 76.5% in the same period—correlation of 0.74.

This suggests that Vale's rally has been much stronger than iron ore's movement. That is, much of the share price performance was justified by macro flow (Brazil as a refuge for equities post sell-off in U.S. equities), it wasn't purely driven by structural improvement in ore, and I wouldn't say it was because of its VBM performance either—albeit strong, but still irrelevant to driving the stock. I would say that Vale approaching ~$17 levels is a scenario where the price is potentially anticipating too much of a move that doesn't seem to be happening in iron ore.

The second problem, which relates directly to the first, is precisely the 2026-2027 scenario for the iron ore cycle, the supply of which has accelerated.

For starters, in terms of structural points, supply is estimated to grow by low-single digits in 2026 (~40Mt)—small in percentage terms, but significant in absolute terms. As the global seaborne base is ~1.7 Bt, an increase of +40Mt would be equivalent to ~1/3 of Companhia Siderurgica Nacional's (SID) annual production or half of Simandou's initial ramp-up. The key point here is that this growth is coming at a time when Chinese demand is no longer expanding. Historically, when +2% supply was the forecast, this was quickly absorbed because the Chinese economy was able to grow at over 3% a year in steel.

Today, this is no longer the case.

In terms of demand, the transition in this scenario would be asymmetrical. Most credible outlooks point to a declining Chinese steel demand trajectory into the 2030s, which makes even low-single-digit supply growth harder to absorb in a scenario of real estate deleveraging, population aging, and migration to service-based growth.

In my view, the structural peak of steel is over—even if there are cyclical stimuli. India helps, being the new bull case for iron ore, although (1) part of production is domestic; (2) logistical infrastructure limits exports/imports; and (3) growth is more gradual. India’s iron ore imports have already climbed to more than 10 Mt in 2025—more than double their recent averages—and are expected to continue rising as steel capacity expands beyond 200 Mtpa toward ~300 Mtpa by 2030.

And taking a cue from Simandou—this being the largest greenfield iron ore project of the last few decades—in 2026, capacity starts to come on stream. The final capacity estimate is 120Mtpa, but in 2026, it will be the first 5-10 Mt, in 2027, more acceleration, and full capacity in 2030. Even if the initial impact is modest, the market will price cycles before production reaches its peak. To make matters worse for producers like Vale, Simandou's high-grade (~65 Fe) ore partially replaces Australian iron ore and puts pressure on premiums. This is a structurally bearish project for the global iron ore balance.

Going even deeper, the problem is not just Simandou. There's Vale itself expanding capacity by ~8-11Mt YoY, Rio Tinto's (RIO) SimFer, BHP (BHP) with increases in the Pilbara, ArcelorMittal Liberia (MT) growing by +15Mt and Onslow Iron (Mineral Resources (MALRY)) increasing by +4Mt. In other words, it's a scenario of simultaneous capacity expansion by major low-cost players.

If we consider that 2026 global capacity is forecasted at ~2.68Btpa, in 5 years the outlook is for an expansion of ~350Mt gross, reaching something close to ~3Btpa. Even assuming natural depletion (of 3.5% per year), the new capacity exceeds the structural loss in the medium term.

The bottom line is that the iron ore market is prone to oversupply. This leads me to view Vale's thesis through the lens that the structural upside is limited, the marginal equilibrium tends to worsen, and the prices may need to adjust lower to restore balance.

When exactly will this happen? Hard to know, but Vale's current valuation already reflects a more constructive cycle than what the medium-term supply-demand framework supports.

After a Strong Rally, Valuation Looks Fair—I Took Profits

Ultimately, in a commodity-driven business, valuation reflects where the market believes the cycle is headed.

In the present context, I believe that the thesis has reached a level that I think is fair. There have been more than 35% gains in the last three months, a 2026 FCF yield falling to ~7%, a trailing EV/EBITDA of 6.7x (above the historical average of ~4x), and an estimated dividend yield falling to 6.95% in 2026 and falling further to 5.4% in 2027.

None of this shows a worsening thesis, just, in my opinion, VALE's price rising and closing the discount it has traded at in recent years.

Even if the Brazilian macro can continue to be a short-term driver for flow and thus keep VALE's multiples stretched for a longer period of time, I believe that fundamental catalysts are missing ahead, and this worries me in the face of a more challenging medium-term iron ore setup.

Given Vale’s high sensitivity to iron ore prices, the stock is likely to revert to a more typical correlation once Brazil’s macro impulse fades.

Therefore, I decided to prune my position accordingly after the recent rally, downgrading VALE to a Hold, as risk-reward now appears balanced rather than asymmetric.
